course.title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
course.description: A primary care sports medicine hip MRI course focused on management-changing interpretation: FAI and the labrum, cartilage, femoral-neck stress fracture and AVN, the abductor/hamstring/iliopsoas/adductor tendons, athletic pubalgia, and red flags.

modules.title: Hip MRI Basics
modules.subtitle: Protocols, planes, sequences, and when arthrography changes management
topics: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip protocol
Planes: axial, coronal, sagittal, and oblique-axial along the femoral neck
Core sequences: T1, PD/T2 fat-sat, STIR, and what each is best for
Fluid vs fat: reading signal behavior across sequences
Non-contrast MRI vs MR arthrography: when labral/chondral detail changes management
learningObjectives: Differentiate a large-FOV pelvis screen from a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol and state which the clinical question requires
Identify the oblique-axial plane prescribed along the femoral neck and explain why it is essential for cam morphology and the alpha angle
Match each sequence (T1 for marrow/anatomy, PD/T2 FS and STIR for fluid/edema) to the tissue question it best answers, and distinguish STIR from chemical fat-sat over a large pelvic FOV
Decide when direct MR arthrography adds management-relevant labral or chondral detail versus when non-contrast MRI suffices

commonMistakes.mistake: Ordering or reading a dedicated small-FOV hip when the pain is poorly localized or possibly referred
commonMistakes.correction: Use a large-FOV pelvis screen first when the pain generator is unclear or extra-articular/referred causes are in play (sacrum, marrow, contralateral); reserve the small-FOV dedicated hip (with oblique-axial along the neck) for a focused intra-articular question.

commonMistakes.mistake: Defaulting to MR arthrography for every labral question, or assuming it is always superior
commonMistakes.correction: Ask whether the added detail changes the next step. Most 3T non-contrast hips answer the labral/chondral question; reserve direct arthrography (optionally with traction) for equivocal cases where confirming a tear or chondral delamination would shift management toward surgical referral.

commonMistakes.mistake: Treating any bright signal on a T2/PD FS sequence as pathologic fluid
commonMistakes.correction: Confirm fluid on more than one sequence: true fluid follows CSF (dark on T1, bright on fluid-sensitive); fat is bright on T1/PD and suppresses on FS/STIR. Magic-angle and chemical-shift artifacts can mimic signal abnormality.

topicContent.content:
### Large-FOV Pelvis Screen vs Small-FOV Dedicated Hip Protocol

The first decision when you open a hip study: **what protocol was actually ordered?** These are not interchangeable.

**Large-FOV pelvis screen** (~32–40 cm FOV, both hips on every slice):
- Best for **bilateral and referred problems**: insufficiency/sacral fractures, sacroiliitis, occult marrow lesions, muscle/tendon injury, an unclear hip-vs-back-vs-pelvis story
- Catches the **contralateral hip** and the pelvic ring you would otherwise miss
- Trade-off: lower spatial resolution — labrum and cartilage are blurred

**Small-FOV dedicated hip** (~16–20 cm FOV, single joint, thin slices):
- Built to resolve the **labrum, chondral surface, and cam/pincer morphology**
- The protocol you want for **FAI, labral tear, chondral delamination** workups

| Question | Order |
|---|---|
| "Where is the pain coming from?" | Large-FOV screen |
| "Is the labrum/cartilage torn?" | Small-FOV dedicated |

If the clinical question is intra-articular, a screen alone will underdiagnose it.
topicContent.pearl: If you are chasing a labral or chondral diagnosis, a large-FOV pelvis screen is the wrong tool — its resolution blurs the joint surfaces; reorder a small-FOV dedicated hip before you commit to a management plan.

topicContent.content:
### Planes: Axial, Coronal, Sagittal, and Oblique-Axial Along the Femoral Neck

**Coronal** — your workhorse for **marrow, femoral head/neck, abductors, and superior labrum**; STIR/T2 FS here is the screen for stress fractures and AVN.

**Sagittal** — anterior–posterior labrum, anterior femoral head-neck junction, and chondral surfaces.

**Axial (true)** — anterior/posterior structures, iliopsoas, and anterior/posterior labrum.

**Oblique-axial along the femoral neck** — slices prescribed *parallel to the long axis of the femoral neck* (off the coronal). This is the **money plane for cam morphology**:
- It lays the **anterosuperior head–neck junction** flat so you can measure the **alpha angle** without obliquity error
- **Alpha angle >55°** = cam morphology (loss of femoral head–neck offset)
- Radial reformats add the clock-face map of where the bump is

Without this plane, a true-axial slice cuts the neck obliquely and **over- or under-estimates the alpha angle**, leading you to mislabel FAI.
topicContent.pearl: Cam morphology lives at the anterosuperior head-neck junction — only the oblique-axial (or radial) plane measures the alpha angle correctly; never quote an alpha angle off a standard axial slice.

topicContent.content:
### Core Sequences: T1, PD/T2 Fat-Sat, STIR — and What Each Is Best For

Match the **sequence to the tissue question**:

| Sequence | Reads | Use it for |
|---|---|---|
| **T1** | Anatomy + **marrow fat** | Normal fatty marrow = bright; **red flag = confluent low-T1 darker than muscle/disc** (tumor/marrow replacement); fracture lines |
| **PD/T2 fat-sat** | **Fluid + edema**, cartilage | Labral/chondral detail, joint effusion, bone marrow edema |
| **STIR** | **Fluid/edema**, robust fat suppression | Stress fracture, AVN, marrow edema over a **large FOV** |

**STIR vs chemical (spectral) fat-sat:** chemical fat-sat is sharper and higher-resolution but **fails at the edges of a large FOV and near metal** (heterogeneous B0). STIR suppresses fat uniformly across a wide field and around hardware, so it is the **edema screen of choice on large-FOV pelvis** imaging. On a tight, dedicated hip, PD FS gives you the crisp cartilage/labral picture.

Think: **T1 = anatomy/marrow, fluid-sensitive sequences = pathology/edema.**
topicContent.pearl: On a large-FOV pelvis or any study near hardware, trust STIR over chemical fat-sat — failed fat suppression at the field edge can hide or mimic marrow edema and send you down the wrong path.

topicContent.content:
### Fluid vs Fat: Reading Signal Behavior Across Sequences

You confirm what a structure *is* by tracking its signal across sequences, not from one image.

**Fluid** (effusion, edema, cyst, simple fluid):
- **T1 dark**, **T2/PD FS bright**, **STIR bright**
- "Follows fluid" = bright on fluid-sensitive, dark on T1

**Fat** (normal marrow, lipoma, fat within a lesion):
- **T1 bright**, bright on conventional (non-fat-sat) **T2/PD**, and **drops out (goes dark) on fat-sat / STIR**
- The fat-sat "drop" is the proof it is fat

| | T1 | T2/PD (non-FS) | Fat-sat / STIR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fluid/edema | dark | bright | bright |
| Fat | bright | bright | **dark** |

**Why it matters:** a **T1-bright** marrow signal that **suppresses** on fat-sat is reassuring fat. A lesion staying **bright on fat-sat** is not fat — characterize it. And **edema-only** marrow (bright on fluid-sensitive, no T1 fracture line) is a different management animal than a true subchondral fracture.
topicContent.pearl: Always pair a fat-sat sequence with T1: a marrow signal that is bright on T1 and suppresses on fat-sat is fat (benign); one that stays bright on fat-sat demands characterization.

topicContent.content:
### Non-Contrast MRI vs MR Arthrography: When It Changes Management

**MR arthrography (MRA)** = intra-articular dilute gadolinium that **distends the joint and outlines the labrum and chondral surface**, letting contrast track into tears.

**When MRA earns its needle:**
- The clinical question is a **surgically relevant labral or chondrolabral tear** (younger patient, FAI, positive impingement signs, considering arthroscopy)
- You need to confirm a **subtle labral detachment or chondral delamination** that non-contrast may miss
- High-resolution **3T non-contrast** can approach MRA for the labrum and is often tried first

**When non-contrast is enough:**
- **Marrow** questions — stress fracture, AVN, transient BME, occult fracture, tumor — contrast adds nothing
- **Tendon/muscle** injury (abductors, hamstrings)
- **Advanced OA** — once cartilage is gone, distending the joint changes no decision

**Management hinge:** order MRA when a positive labral/chondral finding would **push toward arthroscopy or change the operative plan** — not as a reflex.
topicContent.pearl: Reach for MR arthrography only when a labral or chondral finding would change the surgical decision; for marrow, tendon, or advanced-OA questions it adds cost and a needle with no management payoff.

modules.title: Hip & Pelvis Anatomy
modules.subtitle: Labrum, capsuloligamentous complex, the muscle-tendon groups, and variants that mimic pathology
topics: Acetabulum, labrum, chondrolabral junction, femoral head/neck, and articular cartilage
Capsule, zona orbicularis, iliofemoral ligament, and ligamentum teres
Muscle-tendon groups: abductors (gluteus medius/minimus), flexors (iliopsoas, rectus femoris/AIIS)
Hamstring origin, adductors, and the pubic symphysis / rectus abdominis-adductor aponeurosis
Bursae around the hip and pelvis (trochanteric, iliopsoas)
Normal variants that mimic pathology: os acetabuli, supra-acetabular fossa, sublabral sulcus, stellate lesion, herniation pit
learningObjectives: Identify the acetabular labrum, chondrolabral junction, articular cartilage, ligamentum teres, and the capsuloligamentous complex on standard planes

Locate the major periarticular muscle-tendon groups (abductors, iliopsoas, rectus femoris/AIIS, hamstring origin, adductors) and their attachment sites, including the gluteus medius/minimus footprints on the greater trochanter
Distinguish common normal variants (sublabral sulcus, supra-acetabular fossa, stellate lesion, herniation pit, os acetabuli) from true labral, chondral, or marrow pathology
Recognize the rectus abdominis-adductor aponeurosis at the pubic symphysis as the anatomic crossroads for athletic pubalgia

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a supra-acetabular fossa, central acetabular fossa, or normal stellate lesion a cartilage defect
commonMistakes.correction: These are predictable midline/bare-area variants with no surrounding marrow edema; the cotyloid fossa is non-articular and normally contains fat and ligamentum teres. True chondral loss is eccentric, weight-bearing, and usually has subchondral reaction.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reading a herniation pit at the femoral neck as an aggressive lesion
commonMistakes.correction: A herniation pit is a small, well-corticated subcortical cyst at the anterosuperior head-neck junction (T1-low/T2-high), classically benign and often a marker of cam-type FAI; note it but do not escalate to a tumor workup.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling every focal fluid-signal cleft at the labral base a tear
commonMistakes.correction: A normal sublabral sulcus/recess follows fluid signal but lacks the morphology, displacement, and paralabral cyst of a true tear; smooth contrast that does not extend into labral substance on arthrography favors a normal recess. Correlate with the typical anterosuperior location of true tears.

topicContent.content:
### Acetabulum, Labrum, Chondrolabral Junction, Femoral Head/Neck & Cartilage

The **acetabular labrum** is a triangular fibrocartilaginous rim — uniformly **low signal** on all sequences — best seen on **radial and oblique-axial** images. It deepens the socket and seals the joint. The **chondrolabral junction** is where labrum meets hyaline cartilage; this is the **anterosuperior** weight-bearing zone where most pathologic tears and FAI rim chondral damage occur.

- **Femoral head**: spherical, fatty marrow (bright T1). Loss of sphericity at the head-neck junction (asphericity/bump) suggests **cam morphology**.
- **Femoral neck**: scrutinize cortex on T1/fluid-sensitive — tension-side (superolateral) edema or a fracture line is a red flag for a high-risk stress fracture.
- **Articular cartilage**: thin, intermediate signal; assess on fluid-sensitive and (best) **MR arthrogram**.

| Structure | Normal signal | Key sequence |
|---|---|---|
| Labrum | Uniform low | Radial / oblique-axial |
| Cartilage | Intermediate | Fluid-sensitive / arthrogram |

**Management:** anterosuperior chondrolabral disruption with adjacent cartilage delamination shifts FAI toward surgical referral; an intact labrum and cartilage favor conservative care.
topicContent.pearl: Anterosuperior chondrolabral-junction tears with adjacent cartilage delamination drive the surgical weighting in FAI — location of the labral abnormality matters more than its mere presence.

topicContent.content:
### Capsule, Zona Orbicularis, Iliofemoral Ligament & Ligamentum Teres

The **capsuloligamentous complex** stabilizes the hip and is read on oblique-axial and coronal images.

- **Zona orbicularis**: circular low-signal capsular condensation that **waists** the femoral neck on coronal images — looks like a constriction; do not call it a tear or a paralabral cyst.
- **Iliofemoral ligament (Y of Bigelow)**: the strongest anterior capsular thickening; primary restraint to **extension and external rotation**. Anterior capsule/iliofemoral integrity matters after dislocation or hip arthroscopy (an unrepaired capsulotomy can cause **microinstability**).
- **Ligamentum teres**: runs from the **fovea capitis** to the **transverse acetabular ligament / margins of the acetabular notch**; carries a small artery. Best seen on oblique-axial/coronal; tears (sprain → full rupture) cause mechanical pain and can be a hidden source of groin pain in athletes.

**Management:** a full ligamentum teres tear or an unrepaired anterior capsulotomy with instability symptoms warrants arthroscopy referral; an isolated zona orbicularis is **normal** and needs no action.
topicContent.pearl: The zona orbicularis is a normal circular capsular waist around the neck — mistaking it for a tear or cyst is a classic over-call that sends a normal hip to arthroscopy.

topicContent.content:
### Muscle-Tendon Groups: Abductors & Flexors

**Abductors (gluteus medius/minimus):** insert on the **greater trochanter** — medius onto the lateral and superoposterior facets, minimus onto the anterior facet. They are the **rotator cuff of the hip**. On fluid-sensitive coronal/axial images look for tendinosis (thickening/signal), partial tears (footprint signal), and **full-thickness retracted tears** with a fluid gap.

**Flexors:**
- **Iliopsoas**: psoas + iliacus joining to insert on the **lesser trochanter**; the tendon lies anterior to the hip and can cause snapping or bursitis.
- **Rectus femoris**: two heads — **direct head from the AIIS** (anterior inferior iliac spine) and **indirect/reflected head from the superior acetabular ridge/rim**. Proximal strains and AIIS apophyseal avulsions are common in kicking/sprinting athletes.

**Management:** a **full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius/minimus tear** (true cuff failure) is a surgical-referral lesion; tendinosis and low-grade partial tears get rehab. Distinguish an abductor tear from simple trochanteric bursitis — management diverges.
topicContent.pearl: A full-thickness retracted abductor (gluteus medius/minimus) tear is the hip's rotator-cuff failure and earns surgical referral — don't bury it under the label 'trochanteric bursitis.'

topicContent.content:
### Hamstring Origin, Adductors & the Rectus Abdominis–Adductor Aponeurosis

**Proximal hamstring origin**: the **conjoint tendon (semitendinosus + biceps femoris long head)** and the **semimembranosus** arise from the **ischial tuberosity**. On oblique-coronal/axial fluid-sensitive images, document **how many of the tendons are torn** and the **retraction distance** — both drive surgery.

**Adductors**: longus, brevis, magnus, gracilis, and pectineus arise from the **pubic body/inferior ramus**; adductor longus strains are the classic groin injury.

**Rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis** at the **pubic symphysis**: the rectus abdominis and adductor longus blend into a common aponeurotic plate. This is the **athletic-pubalgia ("sports hernia") crossroads** — disruption here produces the **secondary cleft sign** (fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphysis on coronal fluid-sensitive images).

**Management:** proximal hamstring avulsion of **≥2 tendons with ≥2 cm retraction** warrants surgical referral; partial/single-tendon injuries get rehab. Aponeurotic/secondary-cleft injuries go to a core-muscle-injury (athletic pubalgia) specialist.
topicContent.pearl: Proximal hamstring referral threshold: ≥2 tendons torn AND ≥2 cm retraction → surgeon; below that, rehab. Always report tendon count and retraction explicitly.

topicContent.content:
### Bursae: Trochanteric & Iliopsoas

Bursae are normally collapsed; a thin sliver of fluid is physiologic. They become conspicuous (bright on fluid-sensitive, fluid-equivalent) when inflamed.

- **Trochanteric (peritrochanteric) bursa**: lies superficial to the greater trochanter beneath the gluteus maximus/IT band. Distension is part of **greater trochanteric pain syndrome**. Crucially, **always interrogate the underlying gluteus medius/minimus tendons** — bursal fluid is frequently the visible flag for an abductor tendon tear, not an isolated diagnosis.
- **Iliopsoas bursa**: largest in the body; lies deep to the iliopsoas tendon, anterior to the joint. Communicates with the hip in ~15%, so a **distended iliopsoas bursa can be a marker of intra-articular pathology** (effusion, synovitis, OA). Anteromedial location; can cause snapping or femoral-nerve/vessel mass effect when large.

**Management:** image-guided injection helps both diagnosis and treatment, but treat the **driver** — repair an underlying abductor tear; address the joint pathology feeding an iliopsoas bursa rather than just aspirating.
topicContent.pearl: Peritrochanteric bursal fluid is a signpost, not a diagnosis — always check the abductor tendons underneath before settling on 'trochanteric bursitis.'

topicContent.content:
### Normal Variants — Don't Call Them Pathology

These benign findings are routinely over-called. Recognize them to avoid needless arthrograms and referrals.

| Variant | Location | How to call it normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Os acetabuli** | Anterosuperior acetabular rim | Corticated ossicle, smooth margins (vs. acute rim fracture — sharp margins, marrow edema) |
| **Supra-acetabular fossa** | Acetabular **dome (12 o'clock)** | Midline cartilage-filled notch, smooth — not a chondral defect |
| **Sublabral sulcus/recess** | Posteroinferior / anteroinferior junction | Smooth linear cleft paralleling the labral base, **no displacement or paralabral cyst** |
| **Stellate lesion** | **Superomedial** acetabular roof — medial to the supra-acetabular fossa, continuous with the acetabular notch | Star-shaped bare area of absent cartilage, clean margins, no marrow edema — a midline/bare-area variant, NOT the anterosuperior weight-bearing zone where true tears live |
| **Herniation pit (fibrocystic pit)** | Anterosuperior femoral neck | Small T2-bright, T1-dark corticated cyst; common with **cam FAI** but often incidental |

**Pitfall:** a cleft at the **anterosuperior** chondrolabral junction is NOT a safe "normal variant" — that's where real tears live. Location separates variant from lesion.

**Management:** isolated variants → reassure, correlate clinically, no surgical referral. A symptomatic herniation pit should prompt a look for **cam morphology / alpha angle >55°**.
topicContent.pearl: Variant vs. pathology comes down to location: a posteroinferior sublabral sulcus is benign, but the same-looking cleft anterosuperiorly is the prime site for a true tear — never reflexively call an anterosuperior cleft a normal variant.

modules.title: Search Pattern Overview
modules.subtitle: A repeatable, satisfaction-of-search-proof read for groin, lateral, and posterior hip pain
topics: Why a fixed search order beats lesion-spotting
The seven-step hip/pelvis search pattern
Anchor slices: the oblique-axial neck and the mid-coronal joint
Localizing by region: anterior/groin vs lateral vs posterior/buttock pain
Satisfaction-of-search traps and blind spots (tension-side femoral neck, sacrum, symphysis, marrow)
Translating findings into rehab vs injection vs referral vs urgent
learningObjectives: Apply a reproducible seven-step hip and pelvis MRI search pattern to every study
Use pain location (groin, lateral, posterior) to weight the search toward the likely pain generator
Explain how satisfaction-of-search causes missed second findings (e.g., a tension-side stress fracture behind an obvious labral tear) and adopt a habit that defeats it
Convert the read into a management hierarchy rather than an anatomic list

commonMistakes.mistake: Stopping the read once a labral tear or trochanteric bursitis is found
commonMistakes.correction: Labral tears are common and frequently incidental; the obvious finding is exactly when a tension-side femoral neck stress fracture, early AVN, marrow-replacing lesion, or sacral stress fracture gets missed. Finish all seven steps every time.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reviewing only the small-FOV hip and ignoring the pelvic ring, contralateral side, and soft-tissue edges
commonMistakes.correction: Sacral stress fractures, contralateral pathology, marrow disease, and soft-tissue masses live outside the small FOV. Always review the large-FOV/coronal STIR of the whole pelvis and weight the read by symptom location.

topicContent.content:
### Why a Fixed Search Order Beats Lesion-Spotting

The natural instinct is to **chase the obvious finding** — a labral tear, a big effusion — and then quit. That is **satisfaction of search**, and on hip/pelvis MRI it is how the high-stakes lesion gets missed: the eye that locks onto a labral tear glides right past a **tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture** two slices away.

A reproducible order forces you to **visit every compartment regardless of where your attention is pulled**, so the second (often more dangerous) finding still gets seen.

**Why it works:**
- **Coverage, not recall** — you check structures because they are next on the list, not because you remembered them.
- **Calibrates attention** — the dangerous compartment (bone/marrow) is visited *early*, while attention is freshest.
- **Reproducible dictation** — the report writes itself in a fixed sequence reviewers can audit.

**Management impact:** lesion-spotting tends to over-call the symptomatic structure and under-call the silent emergency. A fixed order keeps you from discharging a hip to rehab when the marrow held a surgical fracture.
topicContent.pearl: The first finding you see is rarely the one that changes disposition — a fixed order exists precisely to make you keep looking after you have already found something.

topicContent.content:
### The Seven-Step Hip/Pelvis Search Pattern

Drive every study through the same sequence. **Bone and marrow go early** (step 2), while attention is freshest, because that is where the disposition-changing lesions hide.

| Step | Region | High-yield target |
|---|---|---|
| **1 Verify** | Protocol & question | Side, arthrogram status, oblique-axial neck present, pain location |
| **2 Bones/Marrow** | Femur, acetabulum, ring | **Tension-side neck fracture**, AVN, SIFK, T1 marrow replacement |
| **3 Cartilage** | Joint surface, effusion | Delamination vs full-thickness loss, complex/septic effusion |
| **4 Labrum/FAI** | Labrum, morphology | Tear by quadrant, **alpha angle >55 deg**, rim chondral injury |
| **5 Abductors/Hamstring** | Lateral & posterior | Full-thickness retracted abductor tear, hamstring tendon count |
| **6 Flexors/Adductors** | Anterior & groin | Iliopsoas, rectus/AIIS, **rectus-adductor aponeurosis** |
| **7 Don't-Miss** | Safety sweep + plan | Re-check neck/AVN/marrow/ring, then state disposition |

**Use both T1 and fluid-sensitive on every step** — T1 for marrow replacement, STIR/T2 FS for edema and fracture lines.
topicContent.pearl: Always run bone/marrow as step 2, not last — the femoral-neck fracture and the confluent marrow lesion are the findings that change today's disposition, and fatigue-at-the-end is where they get missed.

topicContent.content:
### Anchor Slices: The Oblique-Axial Neck and the Mid-Coronal Joint

Two slices orient the entire read. Find them first; everything else hangs off them.

**Mid-coronal joint** — the workhorse for:
- **Femoral-neck fracture line** — and which cortex it touches (inferomedial **compression** vs superolateral **tension**).
- **Superior acetabular cartilage** and the superolateral labrum.
- **Abductor footprints** on the greater trochanter.
- Marrow signal of head, neck, and acetabular roof on one image.

**Oblique-axial along the femoral neck** — the dedicated plane for **FAI cam morphology**:
- Anterosuperior **head-neck junction bump** (cam).
- **Alpha angle**, measured here (or on radial reformats): **>55 deg is abnormal**.
- A standard (non-oblique) axial foreshortens the neck and **misrepresents** the bump and angle.

**Before you commit to an alpha-angle call, confirm the oblique-axial-along-the-neck (or radial) images actually exist** — large-FOV pelvis screens often lack them.
topicContent.pearl: An alpha angle measured on a routine axial instead of a dedicated oblique-axial-along-the-neck or radial plane is unreliable — if the dedicated plane is missing, say so rather than report a number you cannot defend.

topicContent.content:
### Localizing by Region: Anterior/Groin vs Lateral vs Posterior/Buttock

Pain location tells you **where to spend your time** — it weights the search without replacing it.

| Pain location | Weight the search toward | Common culprits |
|---|---|---|
| **Anterior / groin** | Intra-articular + adductor/symphysis | Labral tear, FAI, **athletic pubalgia (rectus-adductor aponeurosis)**, iliopsoas |
| **Lateral** | Peritrochanteric | **Abductor (glute med/min) tendinopathy/tear**, GTPS, ITB |
| **Posterior / buttock** | Hamstring origin + sacrum | **Proximal hamstring tear**, ischial bursitis, **sacral stress fracture**, deep gluteal/sciatic |

**Key discriminator for groin pain:** intra-articular (labrum/cartilage) vs extra-articular (**adductor longus origin, pubic symphysis**). They look adjacent but are managed completely differently.

Weighting means you read *more carefully* in the suspected region — it does **not** mean skipping the rest. Lateral pain still gets a marrow sweep; the sacrum and symphysis are exactly where referred pain and second lesions hide.
topicContent.pearl: Groin pain has an intra-articular and an extra-articular answer — read the labrum AND the rectus-adductor aponeurosis/symphysis on every groin study, because the management diverges sharply.

topicContent.content:
### Satisfaction-of-Search Traps (Tension-Side Neck, Sacrum, Symphysis, Marrow)

These are the lesions that **hide behind the obvious finding** and change disposition. Defeat satisfaction of search by *always* completing the fixed order and revisiting these on step 7.

**The classic traps:**
- **Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture** — a *surgical emergency*; easy to skim past once you have found a labral tear. Always state which cortex the line touches.
- **Sacrum / pubic rami** — insufficiency and stress fractures sit at the edge of FOV and field of attention; check T1 marrow there.
- **Pubic symphysis** — edema and the **secondary cleft sign** (athletic pubalgia) are missed when the eye stops at the hip joint.
- **Marrow** — **confluent low-T1 marrow darker than muscle or disc** is a tumor red flag that outranks every degenerative finding. A **lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult without trauma** is a marrow-lesion red flag (pathologic fracture), not a sprain.

**Defeat tactic:** a deliberate second pass dedicated only to bone/marrow and the pelvic ring, after the soft-tissue read.
topicContent.pearl: After you have found the finding that explains the symptoms, do one more dedicated marrow/bone pass — that is the pass that catches the tension-side neck fracture and the confluent low-T1 tumor.

topicContent.content:
### Translating Findings Into Rehab vs Injection vs Referral vs Urgent

Every read ends with a **disposition**, ordered from most to least urgent. Sort the study into one bucket.

**URGENT (escalate today):**
- **Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture** — non-weight-bearing + urgent surgical referral.
- **Pre-collapse AVN** (double-line sign) — the **ARCO collapse hinge**; pre-collapse is joint-preserving, post-collapse (subchondral fracture/crescent) trends to arthroplasty.
- **SIFK** (subchondral fracture line, collapse risk), septic effusion, SCFE, **confluent low-T1 marrow / mass**.

**SURGICAL/ORTHO REFERRAL:**
- **Full-thickness retracted abductor tear**; proximal hamstring tear by **tendon count + retraction** threshold.
- FAI where **rim chondral injury** drives the surgical weighting.

**INJECTION:** focal tendinopathy, bursitis, symptomatic labral tear as a temporizing/diagnostic step.

**REHAB:** **compression-side (inferomedial)** neck stress reaction, **transient BME (no fracture line)**, muscle strains grade 1-2, asymptomatic cam/pincer.
topicContent.pearl: Transient bone marrow edema (no fracture line) is rehab; a subchondral fracture line (SIFK) or a superolateral neck line is urgent — the presence or absence of a fracture line is the hinge between conservative and surgical.
