options.text: Diffuse cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing indicates early/established OA, shifting management away from arthroscopy toward joint-preservation/arthroplasty pathways
The subchondral cysts represent an aggressive lytic tumor requiring biopsy before any treatment
The findings are a focal FAI rim injury that arthroscopic labral repair will durably correct
The pattern is transient bone marrow edema syndrome that will fully resolve with observation

prePostQuiz.explanation: Diffuse bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing reflects OA, which predicts poor arthroscopic outcomes and pushes the conversation toward preservation or arthroplasty pathways. Subchondral geodes in OA are not aggressive tumors (B). The diffuse pattern is not a focal FAI rim lesion amenable to arthroscopy (C). OA with structural cartilage loss is not self-limited transient edema (D).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 52-year-old recreational cyclist has progressive groin pain. MRI demonstrates widespread bipolar cartilage thinning, multiple subchondral geodes, and superior joint-space narrowing, without a discrete focal rim lesion. The patient hopes arthroscopy will restore the joint. Which interpretation best frames the management discussion?

options.text: The geodes are an aggressive lytic process mandating urgent oncologic biopsy
This is an isolated focal chondral flap that arthroscopic debridement will permanently resolve
Diffuse bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing represents OA, moving care toward arthroplasty rather than arthroscopic labral surgery
This represents reversible transient marrow edema needing only rest

prePostQuiz.explanation: Widespread bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing indicates OA; arthroscopy is poorly suited and the discussion shifts toward arthroplasty. OA geodes are not aggressive tumors (A). A diffuse process is not an isolated focal flap fixable by debridement (B), and structural OA cartilage loss is not reversible transient edema (D).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 58-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has 6 weeks of groin pain and a normal radiograph. MRI shows a serpentine subchondral line in the anterosuperior femoral head with a 'double-line sign' and preserved spherical head contour without subchondral collapse. What management does this finding most appropriately drive?

options.text: Reassure and discharge, since the head contour is preserved and no action is needed
Treat as routine transient marrow edema with observation and full activity
Refer to orthopedics for pre-collapse AVN, including protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving options
Start antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis based on the marrow signal

prePostQuiz.explanation: A double-line sign with a serpentine subchondral line in a steroid-exposed patient is osteonecrosis (AVN); identifying it before collapse opens a window for protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving procedures, so referral is key. Discharge (A) or treating as transient edema (B) misses a progressive disease. There is no infectious picture to justify antibiotics (D).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 44-year-old woman with a history of heavy alcohol use has 2 months of groin pain and normal radiographs. MRI demonstrates a serpiginous subchondral 'double-line sign' in the superior femoral head with maintained sphericity and no subchondral fracture or flattening. What management does this finding most appropriately direct?

options.text: Discharge with no follow-up because the femoral head remains spherical
Manage as benign transient bone marrow edema with unrestricted activity
Begin empiric IV antibiotics for suspected septic osteomyelitis
Orthopedic referral for pre-collapse osteonecrosis with protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving consideration

prePostQuiz.explanation: A double-line sign with maintained sphericity in an at-risk patient indicates pre-collapse AVN; catching it before collapse allows protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving surgery, so referral is appropriate. Discharge (A) or calling it transient edema (B) misses progressive necrosis. No infectious features justify antibiotics (C).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 13-year-old boy with obesity has 3 weeks of dull groin and knee pain and now walks with the foot externally rotated. MRI shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial slip of the epiphysis relative to the metaphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Make him non-weight-bearing and arrange urgent orthopedic surgical referral
Begin outpatient physical therapy and recheck in 6 weeks
Allow full activity with NSAIDs and clinic follow-up in 3 months
Inject the hip with corticosteroid and resume sports

prePostQuiz.explanation: Physeal widening with posteromedial epiphyseal slip is a slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), which is an urgent, non-weight-bearing diagnosis requiring prompt surgical fixation to prevent further slip and AVN. Physical therapy (B), full activity (C), or steroid injection with return to sport (D) all risk progression and avascular necrosis.

prePostQuiz.stem: An 11-year-old boy with obesity has several weeks of vague knee and thigh pain and now limps with the leg turned outward. MRI of the hips shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial displacement of the epiphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Begin a knee-focused physiotherapy program and reassess in 6 weeks
Allow unrestricted activity with NSAIDs and routine follow-up in 3 months
Make him non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic surgical referral
Aspirate the knee to evaluate the referred knee pain

prePostQuiz.explanation: Physeal widening with posteromedial epiphyseal displacement is a slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE); referred knee and thigh pain is classic, and the diagnosis is missed when attention stays on the knee. It requires non-weight-bearing and prompt surgical fixation to prevent further slip and avascular necrosis. Knee physiotherapy (A), unrestricted activity (B), or knee aspiration (D) all delay fixation and risk progression and AVN.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 25-year-old hockey player has activity-related anterior groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 68 degrees on the oblique-axial neck plane, an anterosuperior labral tear, AND adjacent acetabular rim cartilage delamination. Conservative rehab has failed over 4 months. What does the imaging add that most supports operative referral over continued rehab?

options.text: The 68-degree alpha angle alone proves symptomatic disease and mandates surgery regardless of cartilage
The rim chondral delamination accompanying cam morphology and a labral tear weights toward arthroscopic referral
The labral tear by itself indicates urgent same-week surgery to prevent dislocation
The findings indicate diffuse end-stage OA, so arthroplasty rather than arthroscopy is appropriate

prePostQuiz.explanation: Associated rim chondral injury/delamination alongside cam morphology and a labral tear is a feature that shifts decision-making toward arthroscopic surgery, especially after failed rehab. Cam morphology is common and often asymptomatic, so the alpha angle alone is not dispositive (A). A labral tear is not a dislocation emergency (C). Focal rim delamination is not diffuse end-stage OA requiring arthroplasty (D).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 29-year-old dancer has deep flexion-provoked groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 64 degrees, an anterosuperior labral tear, and focal full-thickness acetabular rim cartilage loss with delamination at the same location, after 5 months of failed physiotherapy. Which imaging feature most strongly supports proceeding to arthroscopic referral rather than further conservative care?

options.text: The 64-degree alpha angle alone confirms the pathology is symptomatic and dictates surgery
The isolated labral tear mandates emergent operative repair within days
The combination indicates global joint-space collapse warranting total hip arthroplasty
The focal rim chondral delamination co-located with cam morphology and a labral tear favors surgical referral

prePostQuiz.explanation: Co-located rim chondral delamination with cam morphology and a labral tear, after failed conservative care, weights toward arthroscopic referral. The alpha angle alone does not establish symptomatic disease because cam morphology is frequently asymptomatic (A). A labral tear is not an emergency (B). Focal rim loss is not global collapse requiring arthroplasty (C).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 27-year-old with anterior groin pain has a dedicated hip MRI. On the oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck, the alpha angle measures 70 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and cartilage are intact and the patient is currently asymptomatic at rest. How should this isolated finding influence management?

options.text: Treat cam morphology as a common, often asymptomatic finding and correlate clinically rather than refer for surgery on imaging alone
Refer for prophylactic arthroscopic osteoplasty because the alpha angle exceeds 55 degrees
Order an urgent oncologic workup for the femoral neck osseous bump
Place the patient non-weight-bearing for an impending femoral neck fracture

prePostQuiz.explanation: Cam morphology (alpha angle >55 degrees) is common and frequently asymptomatic; isolated morphology without chondrolabral injury or concordant symptoms should be correlated clinically, not taken to surgery. Prophylactic osteoplasty on morphology alone (B) is not indicated. A cam bump is not a tumor (C) or a fracture (D).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 32-year-old recreational swimmer has a dedicated hip MRI for lateral pain later attributed to the abductors. Incidentally, on the oblique-axial neck plane the alpha angle measures 66 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and chondral surfaces are intact and there is no impingement-type pain with deep flexion. How should this isolated cam finding influence management?

options.text: Refer for prophylactic arthroscopic osteoplasty because the alpha angle is above 55 degrees
Treat cam morphology as a common, frequently asymptomatic finding and correlate clinically rather than operate on imaging alone
Order a staging workup for an aggressive osseous lesion of the femoral neck
Place the patient non-weight-bearing for an impending femoral neck fracture

prePostQuiz.explanation: Cam morphology (alpha angle >55 degrees) is common and frequently asymptomatic; an isolated bump without chondrolabral injury or concordant impingement symptoms should be correlated clinically, not taken to surgery — especially when found incidentally. Prophylactic osteoplasty on morphology alone (A) is not indicated; a cam bump is neither an aggressive osseous lesion (C) nor a fracture (D).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 24-year-old runner has deep anterior groin pain with pivoting. The ordered study is a large-FOV pelvis MRI read as 'no fracture, mild bursitis.' You suspect cam-type FAI and want to measure the alpha angle reliably. Which acquisition change most directly enables an accurate alpha-angle measurement to guide the FAI workup?

options.text: Add a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol with an oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck
Repeat the large-FOV pelvis sequence with thinner coronal slices only
Add IV gadolinium to the existing large-FOV pelvis sequences
Switch the large-FOV T1 sequences to STIR for better fat suppression

prePostQuiz.explanation: Alpha angle requires an oblique-axial plane prescribed along the femoral neck on a dedicated small-FOV hip study; the large-FOV pelvis screen lacks the resolution and plane to measure cam morphology. Thinner coronal slices (B) still do not give the neck-axis plane needed. IV gadolinium (C) does not address geometry/resolution. STIR (D) changes contrast, not the plane or FOV needed for the measurement.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 31-year-old soccer player has anterior hip pain catching with deep flexion. Outside imaging is a large-FOV pelvis MRI labeled 'unremarkable hips.' You want to confirm or exclude cam morphology before deciding on a surgical referral. Which protocol adjustment most directly provides the information needed to measure cam morphology?

options.text: Order a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol including an oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck
Re-run the large-FOV axial sequence with a larger matrix and the same coverage
Convert the large-FOV PD sequences to chemically fat-saturated images
Add post-contrast large-FOV imaging through both hips

prePostQuiz.explanation: Cam morphology and the alpha angle are measured on a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol with an oblique-axial plane aligned to the femoral neck. A larger matrix on the large-FOV axial (B) still does not provide the neck-axis plane. Chemical fat-sat (C) changes contrast, not the plane/FOV. Contrast (D) adds nothing for measuring osseous cam shape.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 40-year-old distance runner has worsening anterior groin pain, now with night pain and pain on weight-bearing. The reading radiologist anchors on an incidental small labral fray and stops. Given a fixed search-pattern discipline, which additional structure must be deliberately checked before translating the read into a return-to-run plan?

options.text: The tension (superolateral) side of the femoral neck for a stress fracture line and marrow edema
The contralateral asymptomatic acetabular labrum for incidental fraying
The trochanteric bursa for fluid before scanning the femoral neck
The skin and subcutaneous fat for postoperative seroma

prePostQuiz.explanation: Satisfaction-of-search after spotting a minor labral fray risks missing a superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck stress fracture, which is a surgical-urgency finding that changes a runner from 'return to run' to non-weight-bearing and referral. The asymptomatic contralateral labrum (B), bursa first (C), or skin (D) do not carry the same management-changing red-flag weight in this presentation.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 22-year-old military recruit has 3 weeks of escalating groin pain after ramping up marching, now with rest pain. The MRI report fixates on a small herniation pit and concludes 'benign.' Applying a disciplined search pattern, which area most needs deliberate inspection before clearing the recruit to continue training?

options.text: The opposite hip's herniation pit for symmetry
The iliopsoas bursa for distension before assessing marrow
The gluteal subcutaneous tissues for cellulitis
The superolateral femoral neck for a tension-side stress fracture and reactive marrow edema

prePostQuiz.explanation: Anchoring on a benign herniation pit can mask a tension-side (superolateral) femoral neck stress fracture in a high-risk recruit; finding it converts management to non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral. The contralateral pit (A), bursa first (B), or subcutaneous tissue (C) do not change this high-stakes decision the way the tension-side neck does.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 70-year-old woman has chronic lateral hip pain, a limp, and weak abduction. MRI shows a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus medius tendon tear with 2 cm of tendon retraction and fatty muscle atrophy at the greater trochanter. Prior corticosteroid injection and physiotherapy failed. What does this imaging most appropriately direct?

options.text: Another image-guided peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection as definitive treatment
Reassurance that this is simple trochanteric bursitis managed with stretching
Urgent escalation for suspected septic arthritis of the hip
Orthopedic surgical referral for consideration of abductor tendon repair

prePostQuiz.explanation: A full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius tear with atrophy that has failed conservative care warrants surgical referral for possible tendon repair. Repeat steroid injection (A) does not address a retracted full-thickness tear and may worsen tendon quality. This is not simple bursitis (B). There is no septic picture (C).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 64-year-old woman has persistent lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait. MRI reveals a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus minimus and medius tendon tear with retraction and grade 2 fatty atrophy, after 6 months of physiotherapy and one peritrochanteric injection. What management does this imaging most appropriately support?

options.text: Repeat peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection as the definitive next step
Referral to orthopedic surgery to evaluate for abductor tendon reconstruction
Labeling the process as greater trochanteric bursitis treated with a home program
Emergent admission for presumed osteomyelitis of the trochanter

prePostQuiz.explanation: A retracted full-thickness abductor tendon tear with fatty atrophy refractory to conservative measures should prompt surgical referral for possible reconstruction. Another injection (A) will not restore a retracted tendon. This is more than bursitis (C). No infectious findings are described to warrant osteomyelitis admission (D).

prePostQuiz.stem: A 45-year-old water-skier had a sudden hamstring 'pop' while the hip flexed and the knee extended. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed from the ischial tuberosity with 3 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Provide a home stretching program and clear for return to sport in 2 weeks
Refer promptly to orthopedic surgery for operative repair of the complete retracted avulsion
Provide an image-guided corticosteroid injection at the ischial tuberosity
Reassure as a grade 1 strain and continue normal activity

prePostQuiz.explanation: A complete three-tendon proximal hamstring avulsion with significant (3 cm) retraction is a surgical lesion; timely referral is favored because the window for primary repair is best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring. A stretching program with rapid return (A) or calling it a grade 1 strain (D) understages a complete avulsion. A corticosteroid injection (C) does not address tendon retraction and may impair healing.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 41-year-old man slipped on a boat ramp with the hip suddenly flexed and the knee held straight, feeling a painful pop in the buttock. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed off the ischial tuberosity with 4 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Refer promptly to orthopedic surgery for operative repair of the complete retracted avulsion
Prescribe a home eccentric-stretching program and clear for sport in 2 weeks
Inject the ischial tuberosity with corticosteroid to control pain
Reassure that this is a low-grade strain and continue normal activity

prePostQuiz.explanation: A complete three-tendon proximal hamstring avulsion with significant (4 cm) retraction is a surgical lesion; prompt referral is favored because primary-repair outcomes are best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring. A stretching program with early return (B) or calling it a low-grade strain (D) understages a complete avulsion. A corticosteroid injection (C) does not address tendon retraction and can impair healing.

confidence.statement: I can choose between a large-FOV pelvis screen and a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol, select the right sequences (T1, PD/T2 FS, STIR) and the oblique-axial neck plane, and decide when direct MR arthrography will change management.

I can identify the acetabular labrum, cartilage, capsuloligamentous complex, and the periarticular muscle-tendon groups on MRI and distinguish normal variants (sublabral sulcus, supra-acetabular fossa, herniation pit, os acetabuli, stellate lesion) from true pathology.

I can apply a systematic seven-step hip and pelvis search pattern, use pain location to weight the read, and protect against satisfaction-of-search misses in the femoral neck, sacrum, symphysis, and marrow.

I can differentiate compression- from tension-side femoral-neck stress fractures and state the management of each, recognize the AVN double-line sign and stage by ARCO, and separate BMES from subchondral insufficiency fracture and avulsions.

I can measure the alpha angle in the correct plane, differentiate cam from pincer morphology, identify anterosuperior labral tears and paralabral cysts while avoiding over-calling a normal recess, and recognize associated chondral injury.

I can grade acetabular and femoral cartilage for delamination versus full-thickness loss, recognize early OA, and explain how cartilage status and joint space shift management from joint preservation toward arthroplasty and when MR arthrography helps.

I can recognize gluteus medius/minimus tendon disease behind GTPS, grade hamstring-origin injury with tendon count and retraction, identify iliopsoas pathology and internal snapping hip, and recognize athletic pubalgia including the secondary cleft sign.

I can prioritize the urgent hip/pelvis findings (tension-side stress fracture, septic joint, pre-collapse AVN, occult elderly fracture, marrow-replacing/soft-tissue tumor, SCFE) and route each to the correct disposition.

searchPattern.name: Verify Protocol & Clinical Question
searchPattern.description: Confirm side, protocol, arthrogram status, available planes/sequences, and the exact management question before narrowing your attention. Let the symptom location steer the read.
checklistItems: Confirm correct patient, side, and any comparison imaging
Identify protocol: large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip, non-contrast vs MR arthrogram
Inventory planes and sequences: axial, coronal, sagittal, oblique-axial along the neck, T1, PD/T2 FS, STIR
Localize the symptom: anterior/groin, lateral, or posterior/buttock pain
State the management question: rehab, injection, surgical/ortho referral, or urgent escalation
pearls: Pain location tells you where to spend your time: groin = intra-articular or adductor/symphysis, lateral = abductor/GTPS, posterior = hamstring origin or sacrum.
If FAI or a labral tear is the question, confirm the oblique-axial-along-the-neck images exist before committing to an alpha-angle judgment.

searchPattern.name: Bones, Marrow & Stress Injury
searchPattern.description: Sweep the proximal femur, acetabulum, and pelvic ring first: T1 for marrow replacement, fluid-sensitive (STIR/T2 FS) for edema and fracture lines. This is where the highest-stakes findings hide.
checklistItems: Femoral neck for a stress fracture line, explicitly noting compression (inferomedial) vs TENSION (superolateral) side
Femoral head for a subchondral double-line/serpiginous lesion (AVN) or subchondral insufficiency-fracture line
Diffuse marrow edema pattern: BMES vs AVN vs insufficiency fracture
Apophyses/avulsion sites: ASIS, AIIS, ischial tuberosity, lesser trochanter
Sacrum and pubic rami for insufficiency/stress fracture; T1 review for marrow replacement
pearls: Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture = non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral; compression-side is usually conservative. Always state the side.

Isolated femoral-head marrow edema is a differential, not a diagnosis: chase a subchondral line, a double-line rim, or a transient pattern before reassuring. A lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult without trauma is a tumor red flag.

searchPattern.name: Cartilage, Joint Surface & Effusion
searchPattern.description: Trace acetabular and femoral cartilage for delamination vs full-thickness loss, assess subchondral change and joint space, and characterize any effusion before turning to the labrum.
checklistItems: Acetabular cartilage, especially anterosuperior, for delamination (undermining fluid cleft) vs full-thickness defect
Femoral head cartilage surface integrity and the central/fovea region
Subchondral cysts and subchondral marrow change as load markers
Joint-space narrowing and osteophytes (early OA staging)
Characterize any effusion/synovitis; in the right clinical setting consider septic arthritis
pearls: Hip cartilage is thin; early delamination hides under an intact-looking surface and is the case where MR arthrography (with traction) earns its place.
Diffuse cartilage loss and joint-space narrowing predict poor outcomes from labral surgery and shift the referral target from preservation toward arthroplasty.

searchPattern.name: Labrum & FAI Morphology
searchPattern.description: Assess the labrum by quadrant, trace any paralabral cyst to its tear, and read the femoral/acetabular morphology (cam/pincer) that drives FAI management. Measure the alpha angle on the oblique-axial neck.
checklistItems: Anterosuperior labrum for a tear; distinguish it from a normal sublabral recess or chondrolabral variant
Paralabral cyst as a secondary sign pointing to an underlying tear
Cam: anterosuperior head-neck bump and elevated alpha angle (>55 degrees) on oblique-axial/radial images
Pincer: crossover sign, coxa profunda, acetabular retroversion/over-coverage (LCEA)
Adjacent acetabular rim cartilage for chondrolabral injury/delamination; note os acetabuli and herniation pit
pearls: Asymptomatic cam/pincer morphology and labral signal changes are common: report the morphology, but FAI is a clinical-plus-imaging diagnosis.
A paralabral cyst is your cue to hunt for the labral tear you may have skimmed past; the adjacent chondral status, not the tear alone, often drives the surgical decision.

searchPattern.name: Abductors, Lateral & Posterior Soft Tissue
searchPattern.description: Evaluate the gluteus medius/minimus footprint and peritrochanteric tissues for lateral pain, then the proximal hamstring origin for posterior pain, grading tears and retraction.
checklistItems: Gluteus medius/minimus footprints on the greater trochanter for tendinopathy, partial or full-thickness retracted tear, atrophy/fatty infiltration
Peritrochanteric bursal fluid and ITB / external snapping-hip clues
Proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity: COUNT tendons involved and measure retraction
Apply muscle-strain grading (1 feathery edema, 2 partial with fluid, 3 complete with retraction)
Sciatic nerve proximity to a retracted hamstring stump
pearls: Lateral hip pain (GTPS) is usually abductor tendinopathy/tearing, not primary bursitis: name the tendon, because a full-thickness retracted abductor tear is a surgical referral.
For the proximal hamstring, tendon count and retraction distance decide rehab vs surgical referral, so always state both.

searchPattern.name: Flexors, Adductors & Pubic Symphysis
searchPattern.description: Work through the anterior and groin structures: iliopsoas, rectus femoris/AIIS, adductors, and the pubic symphysis / rectus-adductor aponeurosis.
checklistItems: Iliopsoas: tendinosis, bursa distension, and internal snapping-hip clues
Rectus femoris / AIIS origin: strain, avulsion, or apophyseal injury (direct and indirect heads)
Adductor (especially longus) origin: tendinosis, partial or complete tear
Pubic symphysis: edema, secondary cleft sign, aponeurotic plate disruption (athletic pubalgia)
Differentiate intra-articular groin pain from extra-articular adductor/symphysis sources
pearls: Athletic pubalgia / core-muscle injury at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis is extra-articular and managed differently from a labral tear: inspect the symphysis on every groin-pain study and do not call the normal central primary cleft pathologic.
Anterior snapping hip is usually the iliopsoas tendon: pair tendinosis with bursa distension and the clinical snap, since the snap itself is a dynamic diagnosis.

searchPattern.name: Don't-Miss & Final Management Review
searchPattern.description: Run the safety sweep for findings that change disposition today, then translate the whole study into a clear management next step.
checklistItems: Re-check for tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture, occult/insufficiency fracture, and pre-collapse AVN
Effusion + synovitis + periarticular edema: consider septic arthritis (urgent aspiration)
Confluent low-T1 marrow replacement, periosteal reaction, or a soft-tissue mass: consider neoplasm/metastasis
Skeletally immature: SCFE (urgent) and Legg-Calve-Perthes; consider referred pelvic/oncologic pathology
Summarize: rehab/injection candidate, surgical/ortho referral, or urgent escalation
pearls: Negative radiographs do not exclude an occult or tension-side femoral-neck fracture; MRI is the sensitive test and the disposition can be surgical.

Confluent low-T1 marrow darker than muscle or an intervertebral disc, or a soft-tissue mass, outranks every degenerative finding; do not let satisfaction of search bury it. A subtle adolescent SCFE is non-weight-bearing plus urgent referral.

redFlags.action: A line on the SUPEROLATERAL (tension) cortex is the surgical-urgency pattern — prone to displacement and AVN. Protected/non-weight-bearing + prompt ortho.
A radiograph-negative double-line is ARCO I — the joint-preservation window. Refer promptly and image the contralateral hip (often bilateral).
A subchondral low-signal fracture line (± flattening) carries collapse risk — it is NOT benign transient marrow edema. Offload and refer.
Low-T1 marrow that isn't a stress/edema pattern — tumor or infiltration until proven otherwise.
≥2 tendons torn AND ≥2 cm retraction meets the surgical-referral window (the sciatic nerve sits right there) — flag it before retraction worsens.

surgicalCorrelates.title: Labral tear — MR-arthrography ↔ hip arthroscopy
surgicalCorrelates.body: MR-arthrography is most sensitive for the anterosuperior labral tear; at arthroscopy the same lesion is probed for chondrolabral separation, and tissue quality decides labral repair vs selective debridement.

surgicalCorrelates.title: Cam morphology — alpha angle ↔ femoroplasty
surgicalCorrelates.body: The aspherical anterior head-neck bump (alpha angle >~55° on oblique-axial/radial) is the cam lesion. At arthroscopy it is burred back (femoroplasty/osteochondroplasty) to restore the concave head-neck offset and the dynamic clearance lost in impingement.

pearls.text: An alpha angle >55 degrees defines cam morphology, but measure it on radial reformats around the head-neck junction (maximal between 1 and 2 o'clock) — a single straight axial slice misses the anterosuperior bump and underestimates the deformity.

Cam FAI is the convex bump and loss of head-neck offset at the anterosuperior junction; pincer is acetabular over-coverage. The distinction matters because cam drives anterosuperior chondrolabral delamination while pincer drives circumferential labral degeneration and contrecoup chondral loss.

On radiographs/CT, a crossover sign, posterior wall sign, or prominent ischial spine signals acetabular retroversion (focal pincer); coxa profunda or protrusio signals global over-coverage. Flag these because over-resection of the rim risks iatrogenic instability.

A lateral center-edge angle <20 degrees indicates dysplasia (undercoverage, instability risk) and >40 degrees indicates pincer over-coverage; the identical labral tear is managed very differently depending on which side of that range the hip sits.

Pathologic labral tears live anterosuperiorly and show fluid undercutting the labral base; the key mimic is a smooth posteroinferior sublabral recess following the labral contour. Anchor the call on location, full-thickness extension, and an associated paralabral cyst — not signal alone.
A paralabral cyst is a secondary sign of a labral tear even when the tear itself is subtle; trace the cyst neck back to the labral base on the source images to confirm the communicating tear.

Rim chondral delamination at the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction is the lesion that drives surgical decision-making in cam FAI; look for a fluid cleft undermining cartilage from subchondral bone, which conventional MRI routinely under-calls.

In pincer FAI, search for contrecoup chondral injury at the posteroinferior acetabulum and femoral head — leverage damage opposite the impinging anterosuperior rim — which signals more advanced disease than the rim lesion alone.

MR arthrography with axial traction distracts the joint and opens the chondrolabral cleft, markedly improving detection and grading of cartilage delamination versus conventional MRA; an undermining fluid cleft indicates unstable delamination that reorders the surgical plan. Request it when chondral mapping is the question.

A superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck stress fracture is a surgical emergency: this side is under distraction, prone to displacement and AVN, and warrants urgent fixation referral — not the conservative course used for compression-side fractures.

A compression-side (inferomedial) femoral neck stress fracture without a discrete fracture line can be managed with protected weight-bearing; but a fracture line crossing more than 50 percent of the neck width shifts the conversation toward fixation regardless of side.

AVN's near-pathognomonic finding is the double-line sign on T2 — a hyperintense inner granulation line paralleled by a hypointense sclerotic outer line at the necrosis interface; its presence distinguishes AVN from nonspecific marrow edema.

The hinge in AVN management is subchondral collapse: a subchondral fracture (crescent sign) or articular flattening marks ARCO stage III and ends the window for joint-preserving core decompression, pushing toward arthroplasty.

Transient bone marrow edema syndrome shows diffuse femoral head and neck edema with NO subchondral fracture line and is self-limiting; a low-signal subchondral line instead points to subchondral insufficiency fracture, which carries collapse risk.

Subchondral insufficiency fracture shows a serpiginous low-signal line parallel to the articular surface with surrounding edema, typically in older osteopenic patients; a line length >4 mm or any articular flattening predicts collapse.

Gluteus medius and minimus tears begin as undersurface partial tears at the greater trochanter footprint (lateral and anterior facets) with peritendinous T2 signal — the MRI substrate of recalcitrant greater trochanteric pain, distinct from simple trochanteric bursitis.

A full-thickness retracted gluteus medius/minimus tear with fatty muscle atrophy is the surgically relevant 'rotator cuff of the hip' lesion; measure the tendon gap and grade atrophy, because retraction plus high-grade fatty infiltration predicts irreparability and warrants referral.

Proximal hamstring avulsions hinge on tendon count and retraction: a complete 2-tendon (semitendinosus plus biceps) or 3-tendon avulsion with more than 2 cm retraction is a repair candidate, whereas partial or non-retracted tears are managed conservatively.

In athletic pubalgia, scrutinize the rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis at the pubic symphysis; the secondary cleft sign (fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphyseal cleft) marks the microtear at this common aponeurotic plate.

Internal snapping hip is the iliopsoas tendon flicking over the iliopectineal eminence or femoral head; on MRI look for iliopsoas bursal distension and peritendinous edema, and remember dynamic ultrasound confirms the snap that static MRI cannot show.

Apophyseal avulsions map to muscle origins: ASIS = sartorius, AIIS = rectus femoris (direct head), ischial tuberosity = hamstrings, lesser trochanter = iliopsoas — match the displaced ossicle to the pulling tendon in the adolescent athlete.

A lesser trochanter avulsion in an adult without significant trauma is a pathologic fracture until proven otherwise — search the proximal femur and pelvis for an underlying metastasis or marrow-replacing lesion before calling it a simple iliopsoas avulsion.

A herniation pit (Pitt's pit) is a small well-corticated subchondral cyst at the anterosuperior head-neck junction; usually a benign incidental finding, but in the right setting it flags cam morphology and chronic impingement biomechanics.

Greater trochanteric pain syndrome on MRI is a spectrum — bursal fluid, gluteal tendinosis, and footprint partial tears — and identifying an actual tendon tear rather than isolated bursitis is what redirects refractory cases toward targeted injection or surgery.

Iliopsoas bursa distension communicates with the hip joint in roughly 15 percent of people; a large fluid collection medial to the femoral neck can be a joint effusion decompressing into the bursa — check the joint for synovitis or a labral source rather than calling isolated bursitis.

For suspected FAI, report all three legs of the triad — femoral cam (alpha angle), acetabular coverage (LCEA, crossover, profunda), and chondrolabral damage (anterosuperior delamination plus paralabral cyst) — because surgery must address the bony deformity and the soft-tissue lesion together.

A normal hyaline cartilage stripe is thicker on the acetabular side than the femoral side at the hip; asymmetric thinning, full-thickness defects, or subchondral cysts at the anterosuperior rim signal the advanced chondral wear that lowers the yield of joint preservation.

Ischiofemoral impingement is suggested by quadratus femoris edema with narrowing of both the ischiofemoral space (<17 mm) and the quadratus femoris space (<8 mm); it is an under-recognized cause of deep buttock pain that mimics hamstring or sciatic pathology.

Os acetabuli (an unfused or stress-related rim ossicle) can represent a chronic rim fracture in pincer FAI; distinguish it from a benign accessory ossification center, because a painful os acetabuli may be addressed at the time of rim trimming.

On a hip effusion, a complex or T2-heterogeneous effusion with synovial thickening and rim enhancement after contrast should raise septic arthritis over a bland reactive effusion — aspirate rather than image again, because delay risks rapid cartilage destruction.

items.label: Large-FOV coronal (both hips + pelvis)
items.detail: The screen. T2 FS Dixon across both hips, sacrum, and symphysis catches marrow lesions, occult/stress fractures, AVN, SI joints, and athletic pubalgia — and lets you compare sides. Use this first when pain is poorly localized or possibly referred. At the symphysis, look for the secondary cleft sign; the normal midline (primary) cleft is not pathology.

items.label: Dedicated single-hip axial
items.detail: Anterior and posterior labrum, iliopsoas tendon, and the sciatic nerve. Workhorse for anterior/posterior structures once the question is focused and intra-articular.

items.label: Dedicated single-hip sagittal
items.detail: Anterior head-neck contour (cam bump) and the anterior joint recess / capsule. Profiles the anterosuperior head-neck junction and anterior chondral surfaces.

items.label: Oblique-axial (along the femoral neck) / radial
items.detail: Added specifically to measure the alpha angle. Prescribed parallel to the femoral neck long axis (or as radial reformats) so the anterosuperior head-neck junction lies flat. Cam = alpha angle >~55°. NEVER quote an alpha angle off a straight axial or coronal — it foreshortens the neck and mis-measures the bump.

items.label: Fluid-sensitive fat-sat (T2 FS / STIR)
items.detail: Edema, marrow injury, effusion, stress-fracture lines, and AVN reactive edema. A finding is more convincing when fluid-bright signal points to it — read these on every step.

items.label: T1-weighted
items.detail: Anatomy, marrow replacement (a low-T1 marrow lesion is the red flag), and subtle/occult fracture lines. Fatty marrow is normally bright on T1; loss of that brightness is the warning.

items.label: Dixon — water vs fat
items.detail: Separates water (edema, fluid) from fat in one acquisition. The water image behaves like a fat-sat fluid sequence; the fat image confirms true marrow fat and fatty muscle infiltration — reliable fat-sat across the wide pelvic FOV.

items.label: MR arthrography (the labrum)
items.detail: Most sensitive modality for the acetabular labrum and subtle anterosuperior tears; intra-articular contrast undercuts a tear and distends the joint. Add it when non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam.

items.label: Femoral head & marrow
items.detail: Spherical with uniformly bright fatty marrow on T1. Asphericity / a bump at the anterosuperior head-neck junction suggests cam morphology; a low-T1 marrow lesion is never normal. A radiograph-negative double-line hip is AVN ARCO I (MRI-positive, pre-collapse).

items.label: Acetabulum, sourcil & coverage
items.detail: Smooth subchondral 'sourcil' (the weight-bearing roof on coronal); assess coverage — undercoverage (dysplasia) vs overcoverage (pincer). The cotyloid (acetabular) fossa is non-articular and normally holds fat + ligamentum teres.

items.label: Acetabular labrum
items.detail: Triangular, uniformly LOW signal, firmly attached to the rim — best on radial / oblique-axial. The anterosuperior chondrolabral junction is the weight-bearing zone where true tears live.

items.label: Articular cartilage
items.detail: Thin, intermediate signal over head and acetabulum; assess on fluid-sensitive sequences and (best) MR arthrogram. Look for focal anterosuperior loss / delamination.

items.label: Capsule, iliofemoral ligament & anterior recess
items.detail: Iliofemoral ligament (Y of Bigelow) is the strong anterior restraint; zona orbicularis normally 'waists' the neck — don't call it a tear. A thin anterior recess is normal; capsule integrity matters post-arthroscopy/dislocation.

items.label: Abductors & greater trochanter
items.detail: Gluteus medius and minimus — the hip's 'rotator cuff' — insert on the greater trochanter facets with smooth low-signal footprints. This is the GTPS site; most 'trochanteric bursitis' is really gluteal tendinopathy or a footprint tear.

items.label: Iliopsoas
items.detail: Tendon crosses the anterior joint / pelvic brim toward the lesser trochanter; a small iliopsoas bursa can be normal. Internal (iliopsoas) snapping is a dynamic ultrasound diagnosis, not a static-MRI one.

items.label: Hamstring origin
items.detail: Conjoint tendon on the ischial tuberosity — uniformly low signal at its footprint. Count the tendons: surgical avulsion = ≥2 tendons torn AND ≥2 cm retraction (sciatic nerve is right there).

items.label: Sciatic nerve
items.detail: Posterior to the hip, just lateral to the ischial tuberosity (near the hamstring origin) — trace it on axial images for caliber, signal, and perineural edema.

items.label: Os acetabuli
items.detail: Corticated ossicle at the anterosuperior acetabular rim with smooth margins. Distinguish from an acute rim fracture (sharp margins + marrow edema); in FAI it can represent a chronic rim stress fragment.

items.label: Supra-acetabular fossa (12 o'clock)
items.detail: Midline cartilage-filled notch in the acetabular dome at 12 o'clock with smooth margins and no marrow edema — not a chondral defect.

items.label: Sublabral sulcus / recess
items.detail: Smooth linear cleft paralleling the labral base with NO displacement and NO paralabral cyst. The posteroinferior/inferior junction is the classic benign site; an anterosuperior cleft cannot be called a variant on location alone — smooth margins and absent secondary signs do that.

items.label: Stellate lesion (superomedial)
items.detail: Normal star-shaped BARE AREA of absent cartilage on the SUPEROMEDIAL acetabular roof — medial to the supra-acetabular fossa, continuous with the acetabular notch. Clean margins, no marrow edema. NOT the anterosuperior weight-bearing dome, so it is not where true tears live.

items.label: Herniation pit (fibrocystic pit)
items.detail: Small, well-corticated subcortical cyst at the anterosuperior femoral neck (T1-dark, T2-bright). Classically benign and a marker of cam-type FAI — note it and check the alpha angle, but don't escalate to a tumor workup.

items.label: Where true tears live
items.detail: The ANTEROSUPERIOR chondrolabral junction (roughly 12–3 o'clock) is the weight-bearing FAI shear zone and the prime site for a real tear. MR arthrography is the most sensitive modality.

items.label: Normal labrum / smooth sublabral sulcus
items.detail: Low-signal triangle firmly attached to the rim; a sulcus is a SMOOTH-walled cleft at the base that parallels the cartilage and does NOT enter the labral substance.

items.label: Tear — irregular & undercutting
items.detail: Frayed/blunted/displaced margins with contrast (or fluid) extending INTO the labral substance, often with a paralabral cyst or adjacent chondral loss. A paralabral cyst is a high-yield secondary sign — find the cyst, then scrutinize the adjacent labrum.

items.label: Don't reflexively call an anterosuperior cleft 'normal'
items.detail: A posteroinferior smooth sulcus is benign, but the same-looking cleft anterosuperiorly is exactly where tears hide. Weigh margins, displacement, and secondary signs — not location alone.

items.label: Alpha angle (cam)
items.detail: Measured on OBLIQUE-AXIAL (along the femoral neck) or RADIAL images — NOT the straight coronal. >~55° defines cam morphology (anterosuperior head-neck bump with loss of the normal concave waist). Don't eyeball it on a standard axial; measure it on the neck-axis plane.

items.label: Lateral center-edge angle (LCEA)
items.detail: Acetabular coverage of the femoral head. <~20–25° = dysplasia/undercoverage; ~25–40° = normal; >~40° = overcoverage/pincer (often with coxa profunda).

items.label: Crossover sign
items.detail: Anterior wall projecting lateral to the posterior wall before they meet = acetabular retroversion, a focal anterosuperior pincer pattern. It is an AP-PELVIS RADIOGRAPH sign (confirm on a well-positioned AP film, not on coronal MRI).

items.label: Cam morphology is common and often asymptomatic
items.detail: An elevated alpha angle alone does NOT prove symptomatic disease. Correlate with concordant symptoms and chondrolabral injury before any surgical conversation — morphology is not the diagnosis.

items.label: Femoral version (note)
items.detail: Excessive femoral anteversion OR retroversion of the neck modifies impingement and instability risk; report it when measured, as it co-determines hip-preservation planning.

items.label: Location — anterosuperior
items.detail: True labral tears live at the ANTEROSUPERIOR chondrolabral junction (roughly 12–3 o'clock). Signal extending to the articular margin with irregular morphology, ideally confirmed on two planes.

items.label: Most sensitive modality
items.detail: MR ARTHROGRAPHY is the most sensitive study for subtle labral tears (intra-articular contrast outlines the tear and distends the joint). Use it when non-contrast MRI is equivocal.

items.label: Paralabral cyst
items.detail: A paralabral cyst is a secondary sign of an adjacent tear — trace the cyst back to its labral defect. Its presence raises confidence even when the tear itself is subtle.

items.label: Don't overcall the sulcus
items.detail: A smooth labral recess/sulcus is a normal variant; a tear has irregular margins and reaches the articular surface at the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction.

measurementSections.title: AVN — ARCO staging & the collapse hinge

items.label: Stage I
items.detail: MRI-POSITIVE / radiograph-NEGATIVE, pre-collapse. Double-line sign with maintained sphericity and NO subchondral crescent. A radiograph-negative double-line hip is strictly ARCO I.

items.label: Stage II
items.detail: Radiographic sclerosis and/or cysts appear, but STILL pre-collapse (no crescent, no flattening).

items.label: Stage III
items.detail: Subchondral crescent / early collapse — the articular surface is no longer congruent. This is the management hinge: post-collapse changes the conversation toward salvage/arthroplasty.

items.label: Stage IV
items.detail: Secondary osteoarthritis with joint-space loss and acetabular involvement.

items.label: Double-line sign
items.detail: On T2/fluid-sensitive: inner hyperintense granulation tissue paralleling an outer hypointense sclerotic rim. Highly specific for osteonecrosis; distinguishes AVN from transient marrow edema, which lacks it.

items.label: Lesion size / Kerboul
items.detail: Necrotic-segment size (e.g., modified Kerboul combined-angle, or >2/3 weight-bearing head involvement) predicts collapse risk and lower core-decompression success. The hinge for joint preservation is catching it PRE-collapse — and AVN is frequently bilateral, so image the contralateral hip.

items.label: The distinguishing feature
items.detail: A subchondral low-signal FRACTURE LINE paralleling the articular surface (with or without focal subchondral collapse/flattening) marks subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK). Transient BME syndrome shows diffuse marrow edema WITHOUT a discrete fracture line or collapse.

items.label: SIFK — collapse risk
items.detail: The subchondral line plus any flattening signals structural failure and collapse risk; it is not benign self-limited edema. Protected weight-bearing and orthopedic referral, especially in older/osteopenic patients.

items.label: Transient BME syndrome
items.detail: Self-limited diffuse femoral-head/neck edema that resolves over months. Isolated marrow edema is a differential, not a diagnosis — chase a subchondral line or a double-line rim before reassuring.

items.label: Superolateral (tension side)
items.detail: Tension-side fracture line along the SUPEROLATERAL cortex is the SURGICAL-URGENCY pattern — prone to displacement and AVN. Treat as urgent: protected/non-weight-bearing and prompt orthopedic referral.

items.label: Inferomedial (compression side)
items.detail: Compression-side line along the INFEROMEDIAL cortex is lower-risk and more often managed conservatively with activity modification and protected weight-bearing.

items.label: Look for the line, not just edema
items.detail: A discrete low-signal fracture line within marrow edema (confirmed on T1) defines and locates the stress fracture and changes the weight-bearing protocol. Estimate the percentage of neck width involved.

items.label: Grade 0
items.detail: Normal cartilage — uniform thickness, smooth surface, homogeneous signal.

items.label: Grade I
items.detail: Softening/signal heterogeneity (swelling) with an intact surface.

items.label: Grade II
items.detail: Partial-thickness defect/fissuring <50% of cartilage depth.

items.label: Grade III
items.detail: Deep defect >50% of depth, extending to but not through the subchondral plate.

items.label: Grade IV
items.detail: Full-thickness loss with exposed subchondral bone, often with reactive marrow edema.

items.label: Chondrolabral junction in FAI
items.detail: In cam-type FAI, the surgically significant lesion is anterosuperior acetabular RIM chondral delamination at the chondrolabral junction. Co-located rim chondral injury + cam + labral tear after failed rehab weights toward arthroscopic referral.

items.label: Don't overcall the stellate lesion
items.detail: The stellate lesion is a NORMAL bare-area variant of the SUPEROMEDIAL acetabular roof (medial to the supra-acetabular fossa, continuous with the acetabular notch) — not the anterosuperior weight-bearing dome. Do not mistake this medial bare cartilage gap for a chondral defect; true chondrolabral pathology is anterosuperior.

measurementSections.title: GTPS / abductor tear — the hip's rotator cuff

items.label: What GTPS really is
items.detail: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome is usually gluteus medius/minimus (the abductor 'rotator cuff of the hip') tendinopathy or footprint tear — most labeled 'trochanteric bursitis' is actually gluteal tendinopathy. Name the tendon.

items.label: Grade the abductor lesion
items.detail: Distinguish tendinopathy (thickening/signal, intact fibers) from partial-thickness footprint tear from full-thickness tear with retraction and muscle fatty atrophy.

items.label: What changes management
items.detail: A full-thickness, retracted abductor tear (especially with fatty atrophy) is a SURGICAL referral, analogous to a rotator cuff tear — not a bursitis to inject and forget.

items.label: Hamstring — surgical-referral criteria
items.detail: Count tendons and measure retraction at the ischial tuberosity. Surgical referral when at least 2 tendons are torn AND at least 2 cm of retraction; the sciatic nerve sits nearby a retracted stump. Always state both numbers.

items.label: Repair window
items.detail: The window for primary repair is best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring — so flag the surgical pattern promptly.

items.label: Athletic pubalgia — secondary cleft sign
items.detail: Rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis injury at the symphysis. The SECONDARY cleft sign (fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphysis) indicates the tear. The PRIMARY cleft is the normal midline symphyseal cleft — don't confuse them.

items.label: Snapping hip is dynamic, not static MRI
items.detail: Internal (iliopsoas) and external (ITB over the trochanter) snapping hip are DYNAMIC ULTRASOUND diagnoses. Static MRI may be normal — recognize the limitation.

measurementSections.title: Protocol — when each study changes management

items.label: Non-contrast MRI (most questions)
items.detail: Dedicated small-FOV hip with oblique-axial neck sequences with or without radial reformats. Best first study for AVN, SIFK/stress fracture, marrow edema, abductor and hamstring tendons, and cam morphology (alpha angle).

items.label: MR arthrogram
items.detail: Most sensitive for subtle labral tears and chondrolabral pathology; add when non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam in suspected FAI/instability.

items.label: CT
items.detail: Best for precise bony morphology and version (cam/pincer, acetabular version, femoral version) when osseous quantification will change hip-preservation surgical planning.

items.label: Radiograph
items.detail: First-line and decisive for AVN staging (radiograph-negative double-line = ARCO I; sclerosis/cysts = II; crescent/collapse = III; secondary OA = IV). Also screens for OA, dysplasia (LCEA), and pincer overcoverage, and is where the crossover sign of retroversion is read. A large-FOV pelvis screen cannot reliably measure the alpha angle — order the dedicated hip study for that.

hip-mri-basics.stem: A 19-year-old soccer player has deep groin pain and a positive FADIR impingement test. The ordering note asks: "Cam lesion? Measure alpha angle." The tech is about to acquire a standard large-FOV pelvis screen with true-axial images only.

options.text: Proceed — a true-axial large-FOV screen reliably measures the alpha angle
Switch to a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol and add oblique-axial images along the femoral neck
Add intravenous contrast to the screen to see the cam lesion
Cancel imaging; cam morphology cannot be measured on MRI

hip-mri-basics.explanation: Cam morphology and the alpha angle are assessed at the anterosuperior head-neck junction, which is laid flat only on oblique-axial (or radial) planes off a high-resolution dedicated hip protocol. A true-axial slice cuts the neck obliquely and mismeasures the angle (A wrong). IV contrast does not define cam bone morphology (C). MRI measures the alpha angle well when the correct plane and FOV are used (D).

hip-mri-basics.stem: A 55-year-old with bilateral buttock and groin ache after marathon training gets a pelvis MRI. On the chemical fat-sat coronal, fat suppression looks patchy and failed at the lateral edges of the field, obscuring the sacrum and proximal femurs.

options.text: The chemical fat-sat is adequate; read it as is
Repeat with a small-FOV dedicated single-hip protocol
Rely on the STIR sequence, which suppresses fat uniformly across the large FOV
Add MR arthrography to evaluate the marrow

hip-mri-basics.explanation: Chemical (spectral) fat-sat fails at the edges of a large FOV due to B0 inhomogeneity; STIR provides uniform fat suppression across a wide field and is the edema screen of choice here, ideal for a bilateral stress-fracture question. Reading failed fat-sat risks missing or mimicking marrow edema (A). A single-hip protocol abandons the bilateral question (B). Arthrography is intra-articular and adds nothing to a marrow evaluation (D).

hip-mri-basics.stem: A 16-year-old gymnast with FAI signs has a 3T non-contrast hip MRI suggesting a possible anterosuperior labral detachment, but the surgeon wants confirmation before offering arthroscopy. Marrow and tendons are normal.

options.text: Order an MR arthrogram to better outline the labrum and confirm a surgically relevant tear
Order a STIR-only pelvis screen to characterize the marrow
No further imaging is useful; labral tears cannot be confirmed by MRI
Order a large-FOV pelvis screen to improve labral resolution

hip-mri-basics.explanation: MR arthrography distends the joint and lets contrast track into a labral detachment, adding management-relevant detail when a positive finding would push toward arthroscopy. A STIR marrow screen does not address the labrum, which is already normal here (B). MRA reliably demonstrates labral tears, so further imaging is in fact useful (C). A large-FOV screen has lower spatial resolution and would blur the labrum further (D).

hip-anatomy.stem: A 24-year-old soccer player has chronic groin pain. MR arthrogram shows a smooth, corticated ossicle along the anterosuperior acetabular rim with no marrow edema and intact adjacent cartilage. The labrum is normal. What is the best management implication?

options.text: Treat as a normal os acetabuli variant; reassure and correlate clinically rather than refer for acute fracture fixation
Refer urgently for open reduction and internal fixation of an acute rim fracture
Start a marrow-replacement tumor protocol for an osseous lesion
Aspirate an associated paralabral cyst

hip-anatomy.explanation: A smooth, corticated ossicle at the anterosuperior rim without marrow edema is an os acetabuli, a benign secondary-ossification variant — reassure and correlate clinically. An acute rim fracture (B) has sharp, non-corticated margins with marrow edema, absent here. A normal corticated ossicle is not a marrow-replacing tumor (C), and there is no cyst to aspirate (D).

hip-anatomy.stem: A 62-year-old woman has lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait. Coronal fluid-sensitive MRI shows distended peritrochanteric bursal fluid AND a full-thickness gap with tendon retraction at the gluteus medius footprint on the greater trochanter. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Diagnose isolated trochanteric bursitis and offer only a bursal corticosteroid injection
Refer for surgical evaluation of a full-thickness retracted abductor tendon tear
Reassure that bursal fluid is always a normal physiologic finding
Begin anticoagulation for suspected venous thrombosis

hip-anatomy.explanation: A full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius tear is the hip's rotator-cuff failure and warrants surgical referral; the bursal fluid is the flag for the underlying tendon tear, not an isolated bursitis. Treating it as isolated bursitis (A) misses the surgical lesion. Distended bursal fluid is not always physiologic (C), and the findings are musculotendinous, not vascular (D).

hip-anatomy.stem: A 27-year-old hockey player has insidious lower-abdominal and proximal-adductor groin pain that worsens with cutting and resisted sit-ups. Coronal fluid-sensitive MRI shows fluid tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis at the rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis (secondary cleft sign). Which structure and referral pathway is implicated?

options.text: Ligamentum teres tear; refer for hip arthroscopy
Tension-side femoral neck stress fracture; make non-weight-bearing emergently
Rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis (athletic pubalgia); refer to a core-muscle-injury specialist
Iliopsoas bursitis; aspirate the iliopsoas bursa

hip-anatomy.explanation: The secondary cleft sign at the rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis localizes athletic pubalgia (core muscle injury) at the symphysis — refer to a core-muscle-injury/sports-hernia specialist. The ligamentum teres (A) is intra-articular at the fovea, not the symphysis. There is no femoral-neck fracture line (B). The iliopsoas bursa (D) is anteromedial to the joint, not at the symphyseal aponeurosis.

hip-search-pattern.stem: A 24-year-old runner has an MRI for groin pain. You immediately spot an anterosuperior labral tear and a small paralabral cyst, and begin dictating. Before you finalize, which action best protects against satisfaction of search on this study?

options.text: Re-measure the alpha angle on a third radial image to confirm the cam
Complete the fixed search order, including a dedicated bone/marrow and pelvic-ring pass
Add T1 post-contrast sequences to better characterize the paralabral cyst
Recommend a follow-up arthrogram to grade the labral tear

hip-search-pattern.explanation: Satisfaction of search is defeated by completing the fixed order regardless of the first finding — a deliberate marrow/bone pass catches a tension-side neck stress fracture or confluent low-T1 marrow that the labral tear distracted you from. A is redundant characterization of an already-seen finding; C and D further work up the lesion you already found, which is exactly the trap — they do nothing to surface a silent second lesion.

hip-search-pattern.stem: A 19-year-old distance runner has 3 weeks of activity-related groin pain and normal radiographs. MRI shows marrow edema in the femoral neck with a thin hypointense fracture line abutting the superolateral cortex. What is the appropriate disposition?

options.text: Begin a progressive running rehab program with follow-up in 6 weeks
Image-guided corticosteroid injection of the hip joint
Make non-weight-bearing and refer urgently for surgical evaluation
Reassure that this is transient bone marrow edema and continue training

hip-search-pattern.explanation: A fracture line touching the superolateral (tension) cortex is a tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture — a surgical emergency at high risk of completion and displacement; it requires non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral. A and D would be reasonable only for a compression-side reaction or transient BME with NO fracture line; the line on the tension side is the hinge. B does not address an unstable fracture and could worsen it.

hip-search-pattern.stem: A 31-year-old hockey player has anterior groin pain. The hip joint, labrum, and cartilage are unremarkable. Where should you focus next, given the pain location, before calling the study negative?

options.text: The rectus-adductor aponeurosis and pubic symphysis for athletic pubalgia
The gluteus medius and minimus footprints on the greater trochanter
The proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity
The sciatic nerve in the deep gluteal space

hip-search-pattern.explanation: Groin pain has both an intra-articular and an extra-articular answer; with a clean joint, the extra-articular source — athletic pubalgia at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis (look for the secondary cleft sign) and the symphysis — must be read before calling the study negative. B is for lateral pain (abductors/GTPS); C and D are for posterior/buttock pain — wrong region for this anterior/groin presentation.

hip-bones-stress.stem: A 22-year-old female cross-country runner has 4 weeks of groin pain and a normal radiograph. Coronal STIR shows neck marrow edema, and T1 reveals a discrete low-signal fracture line along the SUPEROLATERAL femoral neck.

options.text: Reassure and allow return to running as tolerated
Order a follow-up MRI in 6 weeks to reassess
Make her non-weight-bearing and arrange urgent surgical fixation
Begin a graded swimming program and recheck radiographs monthly

hip-bones-stress.explanation: A superolateral (tension-side) femoral-neck fracture line is at high risk of completion and displacement with resulting AVN; it is a surgical emergency requiring non-weight-bearing and same-day orthopedic referral for percutaneous fixation. Conservative paths (A, B, D) are appropriate only for infero-medial compression-side injuries, not a tension-side line, so they are unsafe here.

hip-bones-stress.stem: A 41-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has hip pain. MRI shows a geographic antero-superior subchondral lesion with an outer low-signal rim and an inner high-signal line on T2, but the articular surface is intact and contour is preserved.

options.text: ARCO III — refer for arthroplasty
Pre-collapse AVN (ARCO I–II) — joint-preserving options such as core decompression
Transient osteoporosis — protected weight-bearing only
Subchondral insufficiency fracture — treat osteoporosis

hip-bones-stress.explanation: The double-line sign with an intact subchondral plate and preserved contour is pre-collapse AVN — the joint-preserving window — where the femoral head can still be salvaged with treatment like core decompression. On MRI alone this is ARCO I (radiograph normal); ARCO II requires radiographic sclerosis/cysts, and both stages are pre-collapse. ARCO III (A) requires a subchondral fracture/crescent with collapse, which is absent. BMES (C) lacks a double-line and necrotic segment, and SIFK (D) shows a subchondral fracture line, not a serpiginous double-line interface.

hip-bones-stress.stem: A 58-year-old woman, no major trauma, presents with limping hip pain. Pelvic MRI shows a displaced ossific fragment at the lesser trochanter, and the visualized proximal femur and pelvis contain patchy confluent low-T1 marrow that is darker than the adjacent muscle and intervertebral disc.

options.text: Treat as a benign iliopsoas avulsion with rest and physical therapy
Diagnose transient osteoporosis and start protected weight-bearing
Attribute findings to age-related red-marrow reconversion and reassure
Treat as a pathologic fracture and work up for metastatic disease

hip-bones-stress.explanation: A lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult without significant trauma is a classic pathologic-fracture red flag, and confluent low-T1 marrow darker than muscle/disc indicates tumor infiltration rather than normal hematopoietic marrow. This mandates a metastatic/malignancy work-up. A and C dangerously dismiss it; B describes a diffuse-edema syndrome, not an avulsion with marrow replacement.

hip-fai-labrum.stem: A 24-year-old soccer player has anterior hip pain reproduced by flexion-adduction-internal rotation. On standard axial MR images the alpha angle measures 48°. You suspect cam FAI clinically. What is the most appropriate next interpretive step?

options.text: Report no cam morphology, since 48° is below threshold
Remeasure the alpha angle on oblique-axial/radial images angled along the femoral neck
Diagnose pincer FAI instead based on the symptoms
Recommend hip arthroplasty given the positive impingement test

hip-fai-labrum.explanation: The cam bump peaks anterosuperiorly (1–2 o'clock), which a straight axial under-samples and can falsely normalize. Oblique-axial/radial reformats angled along the neck capture the peak; the pathologic threshold is >55°. A is wrong because a single normal axial value does not exclude cam. C is unsupported — the symptoms fit cam, and pincer requires bony over-coverage findings. D skips conservative management and is far premature.

hip-fai-labrum.stem: On a true AP pelvis radiograph the anterior acetabular rim line crosses the posterior rim line cranially, the acetabular floor touches the ilioischial line, and the lateral center-edge angle is 43°. On MRI the femoral head-neck junction is normally concave. Which diagnosis best fits?

options.text: Cam-type FAI
Acetabular dysplasia
Pincer-type FAI
Normal hip morphology

hip-fai-labrum.explanation: Crossover sign (cranial retroversion/anterior over-coverage), coxa profunda (floor at the ilioischial line), and LCEA >40° are all over-coverage findings — pincer FAI. A is excluded because the head-neck junction is concave (no cam bump). B is wrong: dysplasia is under-coverage with LCEA <20–25°, the opposite. D is excluded by three abnormal over-coverage signs. Pincer is corrected by rim trimming with labral refixation.

hip-fai-labrum.stem: MR arthrogram in a 30-year-old runner shows contrast extending into the substance of the anterosuperior labrum with a frayed, blunted margin, plus an adjacent T2-bright lobulated fluid collection along the rim. How should you interpret these findings?

options.text: Normal sublabral recess with an incidental bursa
Anterosuperior labral tear, with the paralabral cyst confirming a full-thickness tear
Contrecoup chondral injury of pincer FAI
Os acetabuli with reactive marrow edema

hip-fai-labrum.explanation: Contrast entering the labral substance (not just tracking smoothly along the cartilage base) with irregular margins indicates a tear, and the anterosuperior location is the classic cam zone. The lobulated T2-bright paralabral cyst is a specific secondary sign of a full-thickness tear acting as a one-way valve. A is wrong because a recess is smooth and does not enter the labral body. C describes posteroinferior cartilage loss, not a labral tear. D is a corticated bony fragment, not a fluid collection.

hip-cartilage-oa.stem: A 24-year-old soccer player has cam morphology and anterior groin pain. Standard MRI shows anterosuperior acetabular cartilage of normal thickness but with a thin line of fluid signal beneath it at the chondrolabral junction. What is the most likely lesion?

options.text: Chondral delamination from cam shear
Diffuse early osteoarthritis
Aggressive subchondral tumor
Normal cartilage, no lesion

hip-cartilage-oa.explanation: A thin fluid signal undercutting normal-thickness anterosuperior cartilage at the chondrolabral junction is the classic cam-shear delamination (the "carpet lifting off the floor") and is arthroscopically treatable alongside cam osteoplasty. Early OA (B) is diffuse and circumferential, not a focal undercut flap. A tumor (C) would show marrow-replacing confluent low-T1 signal darker than muscle/disc, not a surface cleavage line. Calling it normal (D) misses an unstable flap that fails after isolated cam correction.

hip-cartilage-oa.stem: A 38-year-old runner has hip pain. MRI shows a well-defined, rounded, T2-bright/T1-dark subchondral lesion located right at an anterosuperior cartilage defect, with no soft-tissue mass. How should you characterize and manage this?

options.text: Aggressive lytic lesion requiring biopsy
Benign geode confirming mechanical overload
Infection requiring aspiration
Insufficiency fracture needing protected weightbearing

hip-cartilage-oa.explanation: A small, well-corticated subchondral cyst at the cartilage defect/load point is a degenerative geode that confirms a mechanical, treatable problem. The red flag for an aggressive lesion is confluent marrow signal darker than muscle/disc and away from the joint surface (A), which is absent here. There is no rim-enhancing collection or surrounding marrow edema to suggest infection (C), and a geode is a fluid-filled cyst, not a subchondral fracture line (D).

hip-cartilage-oa.stem: A 52-year-old has groin pain and a non-weightbearing MRI suggesting preserved joint space but diffuse cartilage thinning of both acetabular and femoral surfaces with marginal osteophytes. A surgeon is considering hip arthroscopy. What is the best next step to guide the procedure decision?

options.text: Proceed directly to arthroscopy based on the MRI
Order traction MR arthrography to find a focal flap
Obtain a weightbearing radiograph to confirm joint-space narrowing
Repeat the MRI with contrast in 6 weeks

hip-cartilage-oa.explanation: MRI is non-weightbearing and overcalls preserved joint space, so diffuse loss with osteophytes should be confirmed on a weightbearing radiograph; established narrowing (Tönnis grade ≥2) shifts the patient toward arthroplasty and predicts arthroscopy failure, so A is wrong. Traction MRA (B) helps unmask focal delamination in equivocal focal disease, not a diffuse-OA picture. A repeat contrast MRI (D) adds nothing to the weightbearing question.

hip-tendons-muscles.stem: A 64-year-old woman has 6 months of lateral hip pain and a limp. MRI shows a fluid-filled gap at the lateral facet of the greater trochanter with 2.5 cm retraction of the gluteus medius tendon and fatty atrophy of the muscle belly. What is the best next step?

options.text: Reassure and treat as routine trochanteric bursitis
Surgical referral for abductor tendon repair
Repeat MRI in 6 months to assess progression
Intra-articular hip corticosteroid injection

hip-tendons-muscles.explanation: A full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius tear with fatty muscle atrophy is the 'rotator cuff tear of the hip' and warrants surgical referral, especially when atrophy signals chronicity. Calling it bursitis (A) misses the tendon pathology; surveillance (C) wastes the window before further atrophy worsens repair outcomes; an intra-articular injection (D) targets the wrong compartment for an extra-articular abductor tear.

hip-tendons-muscles.stem: A 38-year-old recreational sprinter felt a pop while hurdling. MRI shows discontinuity of all three proximal hamstring tendons at the ischial tuberosity with 3 cm retraction and a fluid-filled gap. Which feature most strongly drives the decision to refer for surgery?

options.text: Presence of peri-tendinous edema
Intermediate T2 signal within the tendon
A 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm
Bone marrow edema at the ischial tuberosity

hip-tendons-muscles.explanation: Tendon count and retraction distance are the two surgical decision metrics: a 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm in an active patient favors operative repair. Peri-tendinous edema (A) and intermediate intratendinous signal (B) indicate tendinopathy/partial injury without quantifying the surgical lesion; marrow edema (D) is a nonspecific reactive finding.

hip-tendons-muscles.stem: A 22-year-old soccer player has chronic groin pain that worsens with sit-ups and kicking. A large-FOV pelvic MRI shows fluid tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis along the adductor longus origin, with edema at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis. The hip joint and labrum are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Athletic pubalgia at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis
Internal snapping hip from the iliopsoas
Anterosuperior labral tear
Femoral neck stress fracture

hip-tendons-muscles.explanation: The secondary cleft sign (fluid extending inferolaterally from the symphysis) plus aponeurotic edema localizes the pathology to the rectus-adductor aponeurosis — athletic pubalgia. Internal snapping (B) is a dynamic iliopsoas diagnosis, typically MRI-negative and without an aponeurotic cleft; a labral tear (C) is intra-articular with mechanical/C-sign symptoms and is reported normal here; a femoral neck stress fracture (D) would show a marrow-edema/fracture line in the neck, not this peri-symphyseal distribution.

hip-dont-miss.stem: A 22-year-old female collegiate distance runner has 3 weeks of progressive groin pain and now pain at rest. Radiographs are normal. MRI shows femoral-neck marrow edema with a linear T1-hypointense fracture line abutting the superolateral cortex of the neck. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic surgical referral
Continue running at reduced volume and repeat MRI in 6 weeks
Begin protected weight-bearing with crutches and routine follow-up in 3 months
Treat as routine osteitis pubis with a core-strengthening program

hip-dont-miss.explanation: A fracture line along the superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck is unstable and prone to displacement with AVN — it is a surgical emergency requiring non-weight-bearing status and same-day ortho referral. Continued running (B) or merely protected weight-bearing with routine follow-up (C) is appropriate for compression-side or edema-only injuries, not a tension-side line. Osteitis pubis (D) is symphyseal and unrelated to a femoral-neck fracture line.

hip-dont-miss.stem: A 58-year-old man with prostate cancer has new left hip pain. MRI shows diffuse low signal replacing the left femoral and acetabular marrow on T1 images; the abnormal marrow is darker than the adjacent obturator muscle and the visualized intervertebral disc. What is the most appropriate interpretation and action?

options.text: Benign red-marrow reconversion; reassure and treat the pain symptomatically
Marrow replacement (metastatic disease) is likely; pursue oncologic staging/workup
Transient bone-marrow edema syndrome; protected weight-bearing only
Normal post-treatment marrow; no further action needed

hip-dont-miss.explanation: Normal hematopoietic (red) marrow stays brighter than skeletal muscle on T1; marrow that is DARKER than muscle or disc indicates replacement and is a tumor red flag — in a prostate-cancer patient this is metastatic until proven otherwise and warrants oncologic workup. Red-marrow reconversion (A) stays brighter than muscle. Transient marrow edema (C) is bright on T2/STIR with preserved T1 fat signal, not confluent T1 replacement. Calling it normal (D) misses metastatic disease.

hip-dont-miss.stem: A 13-year-old boy with obesity reports several weeks of left groin and medial knee pain and walks with the leg externally rotated. MRI shows widening of the proximal femoral physis with metaphyseal marrow edema and slight posteromedial displacement of the capital epiphysis. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Outpatient physical therapy for a hip flexor strain
Continue sports with activity modification and recheck in 4 weeks
Reassure that referred knee pain indicates a benign knee problem
Make the patient non-weight-bearing and refer urgently for in-situ pinning

hip-dont-miss.explanation: These findings are diagnostic of SCFE, which is urgent: continued weight-bearing risks further slippage and AVN, so the patient should be made non-weight-bearing and referred for prompt in-situ pinning. PT for a strain (A) or continued sport (B) allows progression of the slip. The referred medial-knee pain (C) is a classic SCFE presentation, not a benign knee problem, and must not anchor the workup away from the hip.

cards.question: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip — which for which question?
cards.answer: Large-FOV screen for bilateral/referred problems (insufficiency fractures, sacroiliitis, occult marrow lesions, unclear pain source) and the contralateral hip. Small-FOV dedicated hip for intra-articular detail (labrum, cartilage, cam/pincer/FAI). A screen will underdiagnose labral/chondral disease.

cards.question: Which MRI plane is essential for measuring the alpha angle, and what is the cam-morphology threshold?
cards.answer: Oblique-axial along the femoral neck (or radial reformats), which lays the anterosuperior head-neck junction flat. Alpha angle >55 degrees indicates cam morphology. Never measure it on a standard true-axial slice.

cards.question: How do you prove a bright marrow signal is benign fat rather than pathology?
cards.answer: Pair T1 with a fat-sat sequence: fat is bright on T1 and drops out (goes dark) on fat-sat/STIR. A signal that stays bright on fat-sat is not fat and must be characterized. A confluent low-T1 lesion darker than muscle/disc is a tumor red flag.

cards.question: When does MR arthrography change hip management versus non-contrast MRI?
cards.answer: Order MRA when a labral/chondral finding would push toward arthroscopy or alter the operative plan (FAI, suspected labral detachment/chondral delamination). For marrow (stress fracture, AVN, tumor), tendon/muscle, or advanced OA questions, non-contrast suffices and MRA adds a needle with no payoff.
